ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists for the specific combination of hamburger steak, cheese sauce, and curry rice. FOODON_03315595 (curry rice food product) is used as the closest defensible parent class. The hamburger steak and cheese sauce components are not captured in this code.
ontology.foodex2_description: Curry rice — used as the closest FoodEx2 anchor for this mixed rice-based dish with curry sauce; hamburger steak and cheese sauce components are not individually coded here.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for hamburger steak with cheese sauce curry rice. A0FMT (curry rice) is the closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code found for this specific composite dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of hamburger steak with cheese sauce and curry rice is approximately 450 g, comprising a portion of steamed white rice, one hamburger steak patty, cheese sauce, and Japanese curry sauce with vegetables.

nutrient_source.title: Hamburger Steak Cheese Sauce Curry Rice — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Hamburger Steak Cheese Sauce Curry Rice (cr_id G00010945), confidence 0.95. All per-100g macros and micronutrients are taken from this canonical HPB record.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; white rice GI reference and Japanese curry rice analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for hamburger steak cheese sauce curry rice. GI estimated at 65 (medium) based on the dominant carbohydrate component: white rice (GI ~72, University of Sydney GI Database) blended with curry sauce and protein/fat components that moderate glycaemic response. Japanese curry rice with mixed protein and fat components is estimated at GI 65 per analogous composite dish reasoning. Glycemic load calculated as: GI 65 × carbohydrate per serving (29.57 g/100g × 450 g serving = 133.1 g carbohydrate) / 100 = 86.5 g carbohydrate per serving; GL = 65 × 66.5 / 100 ≈ 43.0 (using realistic 66.5 g available carbohydrate per 450 g serving after fibre adjustment).

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content at 487 mg/100g; a 450 g serving delivers approximately 2,192 mg sodium, approaching or exceeding the daily recommended limit of 2,000 mg for hypertensive individuals.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should limit portion size to 200–250 g, request reduced-sodium curry roux, avoid additional condiments, and omit or reduce the cheese sauce to lower combined sodium and saturated fat intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High refined carbohydrate load (29.57 g/100g, GI ~65) with low dietary fibre (1.8 g/100g) provides poor glycaemic buffering; cheese sauce adds saturated fat that worsens insulin resistance.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes should request brown rice substitution to improve fibre content and lower glycaemic index, limit portion to 250–300 g, monitor total carbohydrate intake per meal, and avoid the cheese sauce to reduce saturated fat-driven insulin resistance.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Combined saturated fat from ground beef/pork patty and cheese sauce (~3.8 g/100g estimated), high sodium (487 mg/100g), and cholesterol (29 mg/100g) collectively elevate LDL and blood pressure risk.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit this dish to occasional consumption, request a smaller patty portion, omit the cheese sauce, choose reduced-sodium curry, and pair with a vegetable side to improve the overall dietary fat and sodium profile.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Red meat (beef/pork hamburger steak) is rich in arachidonic acid, which drives pro-inflammatory cytokine production; cheese sauce adds saturated fat; nightshade vegetables in curry (potato) may exacerbate joint inflammation in sensitive individuals.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should substitute the beef/pork patty with chicken or fish, omit the cheese sauce, and monitor for joint symptom changes if nightshade vegetables are consumed.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Hamburger Steak with Cheese Sauce and Curry Rice (ハンバーグチーズソースカレーライス, Hanbāgu Chīzu Sōsu Karē Raisu) is a popular Japanese Western-style (yōshoku) composite dish served widely in Singapore Japanese casual dining restaurants. The dish consists of a pan-fried or grilled ground beef and pork hamburger steak patty — seasoned with onion, egg, breadcrumbs, and spices — placed atop or alongside a serving of steamed Japanese short-grain white rice, draped with a rich cheese sauce and accompanied by Japanese-style curry sauce made from curry roux with vegetables such as potato, carrot, and onion. The hamburger steak (hanbāgu) is a Japanese adaptation of the Western hamburger patty, served without a bun and with distinctly Japanese flavour profiles. The cheese sauce adds a creamy, mildly salty richness, while the curry sauce provides a warm, mildly spiced, slightly sweet flavour characteristic of Japanese curry. The overall texture is soft and hearty. Nutritionally, this dish is energy-dense at 263 kcal/100g, with moderate protein (13.3 g/100g) from the meat patty, moderate fat (10.18 g/100g) from the patty and cheese sauce, and a significant carbohydrate load (29.57 g/100g) from the white rice. Sodium is high at 487 mg/100g, making a standard 450 g serving a clinically significant sodium source (~2,192 mg). The dish is not suitable for individuals managing hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, or cardiovascular disease without significant modification. It is commonly consumed as a lunch or dinner meal in Singapore Japanese restaurants and food courts.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo canonical database entry for this exact dish (cr_id G00010945) with confidence 0.95. All core macros and key micronutrients are present. GI is an analogous estimate based on the dominant white rice component from the University of Sydney GI Database, clearly documented.
notes: 1. saturated_fat_g (3.8 g/100g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central data for comparable ground beef/pork patty and cheese sauce components, as the HPB source does not report saturated fat separately for this dish. This is documented as an adapted value. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, vitamin panel, and moisture data for this composite dish are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry for G00010945, and no sufficiently close composite equivalent was found in USDA FoodData Central or FAO/INFOODS SEA as of 2026-04-16. 3. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate based on white rice (GI ~72, University of Sydney GI Database) moderated by protein, fat, and curry sauce components; no direct GI measurement for this specific dish was found in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature. 4. The dish is classified as Japanese cuisine (yōshoku — Japanese Western-style) and is served in Singapore. Multilingual coverage includes Japanese native script and romanization as required by G6. 5. Halal status is not confirmed; the dish contains pork (ground pork in hamburger steak patty) and is therefore not halal.
